## SECTION 7 — This Day in History (World)
On February 2, 1990, South African State President F.W. de Klerk delivered a massive, historic address to the national parliament, fundamentally altering the massive, brutal trajectory of his nation. He officially announced the massive, immediate unbanning of the African National Congress (ANC) and several other anti-apartheid organizations. Furthermore, he heavily committed to the massive, unconditional release of Nelson Mandela from prison, marking the absolute, definitive beginning of the end of the massive, racist system of apartheid in South Africa.

On February 2, 1943, the massive, unbelievably brutal Battle of Stalingrad formally concluded in a massive, catastrophic defeat for Nazi Germany. The freezing, starving remnants of the German Sixth Army, aggressively commanded by Field Marshal Friedrich Paulus, officially surrendered to the Soviet Red Army. The massive battle, resulting in nearly two million casualties, is universally recognized as the absolute, massive turning point of World War II in the European theater, destroying the myth of German military invincibility.

## SECTION 8 — This Day in History (India)
On February 2, 1835, Thomas Babington Macaulay delivered his massive, highly controversial, and deeply influential "Minute on Indian Education" to the Governor-General of India. In this massive, heavily racist document, he aggressively argued for the absolute replacement of traditional Indian educational systems (Sanskrit and Arabic) with massive, English-medium education. This massive policy shift was heavily designed to create a massive class of anglicized Indians to aggressively serve as clerks for the massive British colonial administration.

On February 2, 1915, Khushwant Singh, the massive, highly celebrated, and deeply irreverent Indian author, lawyer, and journalist, was born in Punjab. His massive, monumental novel, *Train to Pakistan*, remains one of the most highly visceral, deeply tragic, and massive literary accounts of the horrific violence surrounding the 1947 Partition of India. He wielded massive, unparalleled influence over Indian journalism and literature for decades with his highly witty, aggressive, and uncompromising commentary.
